days.title: The Croatian alphabet
days.objective: Learn all 30 letters so you can read ANY word aloud correctly.
days.paretoFocus: Croatian is perfectly phonetic. Master the alphabet once (especially č/ć/š/ž/dž/đ/lj/nj) and pronunciation is solved forever.
drills: Listen to and repeat all 30 letters.
Read 15 words aloud from the cheatsheet.
Drill the 8 special letters until automatic.

items: Recall the sounds of č, š, ž, lj, nj.

activities.title: The 8 special letters
activities.intro: Croatian is 100% phonetic: every letter always makes the same sound, so pronunciation is a one-time investment that pays off for every word you ever meet. These eight letters are the only genuinely new ones. Say each letter, then its example word, out loud.

items.note: Tip of the tongue curled back. Croatians hear č as the 'strong' ch.

items.en: soft 'ch', tongue near the palate, kuća (house)
items.note: Softer and lighter than č, like the 't' in 'future'. If you can't hear the difference yet, don't worry, many natives barely do.
Two letters, one sound. Rare but easy.
The soft partner of dž, just like ć is the soft partner of č.
One sound written with two letters.
Like Spanish ñ. One sound, two letters.

activities.title: Read these 10 words aloud
activities.intro: Two more letters to watch: c is ALWAYS 'ts' (as in cats), never 'k' or 's'; and j is always 'y' (as in yes). Stress never falls on the last syllable, in short words it is usually on the first. Now read each word aloud, slowly, letter by letter.

items.note: h is a light 'kh' as in 'loch'. Pronounce both h and v.
j = 'y', always.
c = 'ts', never 'k'.
k + nj, pronounce both.
The r acts as a vowel between consonants: Hr-vat-ska. Same in prst (finger) and trg (square).

activities.title: Alphabet practice
activities.intro: Recognize the sounds first, then spell words yourself. Type the diacritics (č, ć, š, ž, đ) they are part of the letter, not decoration.

questions.prompt: Which letter sounds like 'sh' in shoe?
questions.explanation: š = 'sh' (škola = school). ž is 'zh', č is 'ch'.

questions.prompt: Which letter sounds like 'ny' in canyon?
questions.explanation: nj = 'ny' (konj = horse). lj is 'ly' as in million.

questions.prompt: How is the letter c ALWAYS pronounced in Croatian?
questions.explanation: Croatian c is always 'ts': cipela = TSEE-peh-lah. For the 'ch' sounds Croatian uses č and ć.

questions.prompt: The word 'ljubav' (love) starts with which sound?
questions.explanation: lj is ONE sound written with two letters, like the 'lli' in million.

questions.prompt: Write the single letter that sounds like the 's' in 'pleasure'.
questions.explanation: ž = 'zh' as in pleasure: žena (woman). Without the hook, z is a plain 'z'.

questions.prompt: Spell the Croatian word for 'tea' (starts with the hard 'ch' letter).
questions.explanation: čaj, č for the hard 'ch', j for the 'y' glide at the end.

questions.prompt: Spell the Croatian word for 'school' (you heard it as SHKOH-lah).
questions.explanation: škola, the 'sh' sound is always written š. Spelling = sound, always.

questions.prompt: Spell the Croatian word for 'house' (KOO-chah, with the SOFT ch).
questions.explanation: kuća takes ć, the soft ch. čaj takes č, the hard ch. Learning which word takes which comes with exposure, but the sound rule never changes.

questions.prompt: Where does stress NEVER fall in a Croatian word?
questions.explanation: Stress never falls on the last syllable. In short words it is usually on the first: DObro, HVAla, ŠKOla.

activities.title: Pronunciation game with your partner
activities.intro: Read your lines aloud slowly. Your partner listens and corrects your č/ć, š/ž, lj/nj. Swap roles afterwards if you like.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Čaj, kuća, škola, žena, ljubav, konj.
lines.en: Tea, house, school, woman, love, horse.

days.title: Greetings & politeness
days.paretoFocus: A few fixed phrases (bok, dobar dan, hvala, molim) cover almost every first interaction.
drills: Learn bok / dobar dan / dobro jutro / doviđenja.
Practise hvala / molim / oprostite / da / ne.
Role-play greeting someone formally and casually.

activities.title: Greetings for every moment of the day
activities.intro: Croatian greetings split cleanly into informal (friends, family, your partner) and standard/formal (strangers, shops, older people). When in doubt, use the formal one, nobody is ever offended by Dobar dan.

items.note: Friends and family only. Works both when arriving and leaving.
Used until roughly 10 a.m.
Your safe default with anyone you don't know. Note: dobAR dan but dobrO jutro, the adjective matches the noun.
Only when parting for the night, not a greeting.
Literally 'until seeing (each other)'.

items.en: How are you? (formal, or to several people)

items.note: Formal version: A vi?

activities.title: The politeness toolkit
activities.intro: These little words carry almost every polite interaction. Molim is the hardest worker in Croatian, it means please, you're welcome, AND 'pardon?' when you didn't hear.

items.note: Stronger: hvala lijepa (thanks a lot).
Three uses: Molim! (here you go / you're welcome), Molim? (sorry, what did you say?), and please in requests.
Use to get a stranger's attention or apologize. Informal: oprosti.

items.en: here you are / how can I help you? (formal)
items.note: What waiters and shopkeepers say to you; also said when handing something over.
Literally 'in order'.

activities.title: Greetings practice
activities.intro: Pick or produce the right greeting for each situation. Watch the diacritics in doviđenja and večer.

questions.prompt: You walk into a bakery at noon. What do you say?
questions.explanation: Dobar dan is the standard daytime greeting with strangers. Bok is only for friends and family.

questions.prompt: It's 8 a.m. and you greet your neighbour. What fits best?
questions.explanation: Dobro jutro is used until roughly 10 a.m.; after that switch to Dobar dan.

questions.prompt: When can you say 'Laku noć'?
options: Only when parting for the night
Any time after dark, including when arriving
questions.answer: Only when parting for the night
questions.explanation: Laku noć is a farewell, not a greeting. Arriving in the evening you say Dobra večer.

questions.prompt: Someone thanks you: 'Hvala!' You reply:
questions.explanation: Molim also means 'you're welcome'. It triples as please and 'pardon?'.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'thank you'
questions.explanation: hvala, pronounce both the h and the v: HVAH-lah.

questions.prompt: You need to stop a stranger on the street to ask something. Say 'excuse me' (formal).
questions.explanation: oprostite is the formal 'excuse me / sorry'. To a friend you'd say oprosti.

questions.prompt: Translate the formal goodbye: 'goodbye'
questions.explanation: doviđenja, note the đ (soft j). Literally 'until seeing each other'. Informal alternative: vidimo se.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'Good day, how are you?' (formal)
questions.explanation: Formal greeting + formal 'how are you' (ste, not si). This exact sentence opens most polite conversations.

questions.prompt: Build the reply: 'I'm fine, thanks. And you?' (informal)
questions.explanation: Dobro sam = I'm fine. A ti? bounces the question back informally; formally you'd say A vi?

activities.title: Role-play: formal, then casual
activities.intro: Act this out with your partner. Round 1: your partner plays an older neighbour (formal). Round 2: they play themselves (casual). Notice how ste/vi becomes si/ti.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: I ja sam dobro, hvala. Doviđenja!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bok! Dobro sam, hvala. A ti?
lines.en: Hi! I'm fine, thanks. And you?

days.title: Introduce yourself
days.objective: Say your name and nationality and ask the same.
days.paretoFocus: 'Ja sam… / Kako se zoveš?' opens any conversation. Maximum reuse for minimal effort.
drills: Build your intro: Ja sam… (name, nationality).
Learn Kako se zoveš? / Odakle si?
Say your intro aloud 5 times.

items: Give your full intro from memory.

activities.title: Introducing yourself
activities.intro: Two ways to give your name: Ja sam + name (I am...) or Zovem se + name (I'm called...). Both are equally common. Remember the informal/formal split from yesterday: zoveš/si to a friend, zovete/ste to a stranger.

items.note: The simplest intro: Ja sam + your name.

items.en: My name is Ana. (literally: I call myself Ana)
Nice to meet you. (literally: it is dear to me)
items.note: Said while shaking hands. The standard reply is also Drago mi je.
Learn 'iz Amerike' as a chunk, after iz the ending changes (Amerika → Amerike). You'll learn that pattern later; for now memorize the phrase.
Nationalities have male/female pairs: Amerikanac/Amerikanka, Hrvat/Hrvatica.

items.hr: On je Hrvat. Ona je Hrvatica.
items.en: He is Croatian. She is Croatian.

items.note: The easiest way to keep a conversation going: answer, then bounce it back.

activities.title: Introduction practice
activities.intro: First recognize the phrases, then produce your own intro. Use your real name where a name is needed, accepted answers use Ana as the model.

questions.prompt: What does 'Kako se zoveš?' mean?
questions.explanation: Kako se zoveš? = literally 'how do you call yourself?', the informal way to ask a name.

questions.prompt: You're introduced to your partner's grandmother. How do you ask HER name?
questions.explanation: Older person you've just met → formal: Kako se zovete? The -te ending marks the polite vi form.

questions.prompt: A woman from Croatia says:
questions.explanation: Hrvatica = Croatian woman, Hrvat = Croatian man, Hrvatska = the country Croatia, hrvatski = the language.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'Nice to meet you.'
questions.explanation: Drago mi je, literally 'it is dear to me'. Say it while shaking hands.

questions.prompt: Ask a new friend (informal): 'Where are you from?'
questions.explanation: Odakle si? is informal; to a stranger you'd say Odakle ste?

questions.prompt: Say 'My name is Ana.' using zvati se.
questions.explanation: Zovem se + name. The little word se always comes right after zovem here.

questions.prompt: Build the question: 'What's your name?' (informal)
questions.explanation: Kako (how) + se zoveš (do you call yourself). The se sits in second position.

questions.prompt: Build the sentence: 'I am from America.'
questions.explanation: Ja sam + iz Amerike (memorized chunk, Amerika changes to Amerike after iz).

activities.title: Role-play: meeting someone new
activities.intro: Your partner plays Marko, a friend of the family you're meeting for the first time. Use your own name and nationality in place of Alex / iz Amerike. Repeat until it flows without reading.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Bok! Zovem se Alex. Drago mi je.
lines.en: Hi! My name is Alex. Nice to meet you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Drago mi je. Odakle si, Alex?
lines.en: Nice to meet you. Where are you from, Alex?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja sam iz Amerike. A ti? Odakle si?
lines.en: I am from America. And you? Where are you from?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ja sam iz Zagreba. Ja sam Hrvat.
lines.en: I am from Zagreb. I am Croatian.

days.title: Three genders
days.objective: Recognise masculine, feminine and neuter nouns by their endings.
days.paretoFocus: Unlike many languages, Croatian gender is usually visible in the ending, a huge shortcut you should exploit from day one.
drills: Learn the endings: consonant (m), -a (f), -o/-e (n).
Sort 12 nouns by gender.
Note: there are NO articles in Croatian.

items: Guess the gender of 8 new nouns.
Why is there no 'the' in Croatian?

activities.title: Three genders, read the ending
activities.intro: Every Croatian noun is masculine, feminine or neuter, and the ending usually tells you which: consonant → masculine, -a → feminine, -o/-e → neuter. And great news: there are NO articles. Žena means woman, a woman, AND the woman.

items.note: Ends in a consonant → masculine.
Ends in -a → feminine.
Ends in -o → neuter.
Ends in -e → neuter.
A small group of feminine nouns ends in a consonant: noć, ljubav (love), stvar (thing). Learn these as exceptions.

activities.title: Sort the genders
activities.intro: Look at the ending and call the gender. This skill becomes automatic fast, and everything later (adjectives, cases) builds on it.

questions.prompt: What gender is 'kava' (coffee)?
questions.explanation: Ends in -a → feminine.

questions.prompt: What gender is 'stol' (table)?
questions.explanation: Ends in a consonant → masculine.

questions.prompt: What gender is 'more' (sea)?
questions.explanation: Ends in -e → neuter (like mlijeko and selo, which end in -o).

questions.prompt: Which of these nouns is feminine?
questions.explanation: škola ends in -a → feminine. grad and kruh are masculine (consonant), selo is neuter (-o).

questions.prompt: Which of these nouns is NEUTER?
questions.explanation: jutro ends in -o → neuter. kuća is feminine, prijatelj masculine, noć feminine (exception).

activities.title: No articles, and the tricky ones
activities.intro: Now produce the words yourself and handle the exceptions.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'THE woman' in Croatian?
questions.explanation: Croatian has NO articles. žena covers 'woman', 'a woman' and 'the woman', context does the rest.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'milk' (a neuter noun)
questions.explanation: mlijeko, the -o ending marks it as neuter. Watch the ije in the middle.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'house' (a feminine noun)
questions.explanation: kuća, feminine because of -a. Soft ć, not č.

questions.prompt: 'noć' (night) ends in a consonant. What gender is it?
questions.explanation: A small group of feminine nouns ends in a consonant: noć, ljubav, stvar. Memorize them as you meet them; the consonant rule covers everything else.

questions.prompt: Why does Croatian have no word for 'the'?
options: Definiteness comes from context and word order, so articles aren't needed
It was abolished by a spelling reform
Croatian actually has articles, they're just optional
questions.answer: Definiteness comes from context and word order, so articles aren't needed
questions.explanation: Like most Slavic languages, Croatian never developed articles. Context (and later, word order and case endings) makes clear whether you mean 'a' or 'the'.

days.title: Personal pronouns
days.objective: Know ja, ti, on/ona/ono, mi, vi, oni/one.
days.paretoFocus: Pronouns frame every verb you'll learn, and Croatian often drops them because verb endings already show the subject.
drills: Chant the pronouns in order.
Note ti (informal) vs vi (formal/plural).
Understand why pronouns are often omitted.

items: Choose ti/vi for: a friend, a stranger, a group.

activities.title: The personal pronouns
activities.intro: Nine little words frame every verb you'll ever learn. Chant them in order until they're automatic: ja, ti, then the third-person trio on, ona, ono, then plural mi, vi, and finally oni, one.

items.note: For neuter nouns like dijete (child) or selo (village).

items.en: you (several people, OR one person formally)
items.note: Often written Vi when formal.
One man in the group is enough to make it oni.

activities.title: ti vs vi, and why pronouns vanish
activities.intro: Two things make Croatian pronouns special: the politeness split (ti/vi), and the fact that they're usually dropped, because the verb ending already shows who is acting.

items.en: for friends, family, children, your partner
items.note: Using ti with a stranger can feel rude, like being overly familiar.

items.en: for strangers, officials, older people, shop staff
items.note: Also the plain plural 'you all'. When unsure, start with vi, Croatians will offer ti when ready ('možemo na ti').
The verb form sam already means 'I am', adding ja is only for emphasis.
Keep the pronoun when you contrast: me vs you.
The -m ending says 'I'. This is why Croatian sentences often start straight with the verb.

activities.title: Pronoun practice
activities.intro: Recognize each pronoun, choose ti or vi correctly, and understand the dropping rule.

questions.prompt: Which pronoun means 'we'?
questions.explanation: mi = we. Don't confuse it with vi (you plural/formal).

questions.prompt: Translate: 'she'
questions.explanation: ona = she. (on = he, ono = it.)

questions.prompt: Translate: 'he'
questions.explanation: on = he. Add -a for she (ona), -o for it (ono).

questions.prompt: A group of five women, 'they' is:
questions.explanation: one = they (all women). If even one man joins the group, it becomes oni.

questions.prompt: You're asking your partner's best friend (your age, you've met many times) a question. Which 'you'?
questions.explanation: Friends and peers → ti. vi would sound oddly distant here.

questions.prompt: You're addressing a police officer. Which 'you'?
questions.explanation: Officials, strangers, older people → vi (formal). When unsure, vi is always the safe choice.

questions.prompt: You're speaking to a group of three friends at once. Which 'you'?
questions.explanation: vi is also the plain plural, any group gets vi, no matter how informal.

questions.prompt: Why can Croatian drop pronouns ('Učim hrvatski' instead of 'Ja učim hrvatski')?
options: The verb ending already shows who the subject is
It's slang, the full form is required in writing
questions.answer: The verb ending already shows who the subject is
questions.explanation: The -m in učim can only mean 'I', so ja is redundant. Keep the pronoun only for emphasis or contrast: JA učim, a ti ne.

days.title: biti (to be), present
days.objective: Use the short and negative forms of 'to be'.
days.paretoFocus: biti is the most common verb and the helper for the past tense later. The short forms (sam, si, je…) appear in nearly every sentence.
drills: Learn sam, si, je, smo, ste, su.
Learn the negatives: nisam, nisi, nije…
Make 5 sentences: Ja sam…, Ona je…, Mi smo…

activities.title: biti (to be): the short forms
activities.intro: biti is the most common Croatian verb and appears in nearly every sentence. In normal speech you use the SHORT forms: sam, si, je, smo, ste, su. One rule: a short form can never be the FIRST word of a sentence, say 'Ja sam Ana' or 'Ana sam', never 'Sam Ana'.

items.note: Ja sam umoran. = I am tired. (man speaking; a woman says umorna)
Ti si student. = You are a student.
Ona je učiteljica. = She is a teacher.
Mi smo prijatelji. = We are friends.
Vi ste iz Zagreba? = You are from Zagreb?
Oni su ovdje. = They are here.
With ja dropped, sam moves to second position, it can never stand first.

activities.title: biti: the negative forms
activities.intro: The negative of biti is a single word, ne + the form fuse together: nisam, nisi, nije... Unlike the short forms, negatives CAN start a sentence: Nisam umoran.

items.note: Nisam iz Zagreba. = I'm not from Zagreb.
Ona nije ovdje. = She is not here.
Oni nisu studenti. = They are not students.

activities.title: biti practice
activities.intro: Fill in the right form of biti (positive and negative) then build full sentences.

questions.prompt: Ja ___ student. (I am a student.)
questions.explanation: ja → sam. The short form used in normal sentences.

questions.prompt: Ti ___ moj prijatelj. (You are my friend.)
questions.explanation: ti → si.

questions.prompt: Mi ___ iz Amerike. (We are from America.)
questions.explanation: mi → smo. (vi → ste, oni → su.)

questions.prompt: Oni ___ ovdje. (They are here.)
questions.explanation: oni → su, the third-person plural short form.

questions.prompt: Make it negative: 'Ja sam umoran' → 'Ja ___ umoran.' (I am NOT tired.)
questions.explanation: ne + sam fuse into one word: nisam.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is correct?
questions.explanation: The short form sam can never be the FIRST word. Ja sam student. and Student sam. are both fine; Sam student. is impossible.

questions.prompt: Build: 'She is not here.'
questions.explanation: ona → nije (negative of je). Negative forms are free to stand anywhere, even first: Nije ovdje.

questions.prompt: Build: 'We are friends.'
questions.explanation: mi → smo; prijatelj takes the plural -i: prijatelji.

questions.prompt: Translate the whole sentence: 'She is not here.'
questions.explanation: Ona nije ovdje. and because the ending shows the subject, Nije ovdje. alone is also perfectly natural.

days.title: Week 1 review + numbers 0-20
days.paretoFocus: Weekly consolidation locks in gains; numbers are pure daily utility for prices, time and quantities.
drills: Count 0-20 aloud twice.
Clear your due words in the Words tab.
Free-talk 2 minutes about yourself.

activities.title: Numbers 0-10
activities.intro: Numbers are pure daily utility, prices, times, quantities. Read each one aloud twice. Every letter is pronounced exactly as you learned on lesson 1.

items.note: Starts with č, CHEH-tee-ree.
š = 'sh': shest.

activities.title: Numbers 11-20
activities.intro: 11-19 follow one pattern: a squeezed form of the digit + -naest (jedan → jedanaest). Spot the digit inside each teen and you've learned them all at once.

items.note: četiri squeezes to četr-.
šest drops its t: šes-naest.
The tens all work this way: trideset 30, četrdeset 40...

activities.title: Numbers + week 1 review
activities.intro: Numbers first, then a sweep back through the whole week: greetings, introductions, genders, biti.

questions.prompt: What number is 'osam'?
questions.explanation: osam = 8. (sedam = 7, osamnaest = 18.)

questions.prompt: Write the number 5 in Croatian.
questions.explanation: pet = 5. petnaest = 15.

questions.prompt: Which is 17?
questions.explanation: sedam (7) + naest = sedamnaest (17). sedamdeset is 70.

questions.prompt: Write the number 14 in Croatian.
questions.explanation: četiri squeezes to četr- before -naest: četrnaest. Don't forget the č.

questions.prompt: Write the number 20 in Croatian.
questions.explanation: dva + deset = 'two tens' = dvadeset.

questions.prompt: REVIEW: You meet your partner's boss for the first time at 2 p.m. You say:
questions.explanation: Formal situation, daytime → Dobar dan, plus Drago mi je when being introduced. Bok and ti-forms are for friends.

questions.prompt: REVIEW: What gender is 'selo' (village)?
questions.explanation: Ends in -o → neuter, like mlijeko and jutro.

questions.prompt: REVIEW: Vi ___ iz Hrvatske? (You are from Croatia? formal)
questions.explanation: vi → ste. The whole biti row: sam, si, je, smo, ste, su.

questions.prompt: REVIEW: Build your one-line introduction: 'Good day, I am Ana.'
questions.explanation: Greeting + Ja sam + name: everything from lessons 2 and 3 in one breath.

activities.title: Free talk: about yourself, plus a numbers game
activities.intro: The week 1 victory lap. Act it out with your partner, use your own name and nationality. In the numbers round, your partner can invent new sums; answer in Croatian.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Super! Tko si ti? Reci mi!
lines.en: Great! Who are you? Tell me!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja sam Alex. Amerikanac sam i učim hrvatski.
lines.en: I am Alex. I'm American and I'm learning Croatian. (woman: Amerikanka sam)

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Bravo! Sada brojevi. Koliko je dva plus tri?
lines.en: Bravo! Now numbers. How much is two plus three?

days.title: Present tense verb endings
days.objective: Conjugate regular verbs in the present.
days.paretoFocus: One set of endings (-m, -š, (none), -mo, -te, -ju/-e) conjugates most verbs. The ending alone shows who's acting.
drills: Learn raditi: radim, radiš, radi, radimo, radite, rade.
Conjugate govoriti and učiti.
Write 6 sentences with different verbs.

activities.title: raditi (to work): the pattern
activities.intro: One set of endings conjugates most Croatian verbs: -m, -š, (nothing), -mo, -te, -e/-ju. Drop the infinitive's -ti, add the ending, done. And just like with biti, the ending alone shows WHO, so pronouns are optional.

items.note: raditi minus -ti → radi- + m.
Third person singular has no ending, just the stem.
-i verbs use -e for 'they'. -a verbs use -ju: gledati → gledaju (they watch).

activities.title: govoriti (to speak) & učiti (to learn), same music
activities.intro: Two more high-value verbs, same endings. Once you feel the pattern, you can conjugate hundreds of verbs.

items.note: Govorim malo hrvatski. = I speak a little Croatian, your most useful sentence this month.
Govoriš li engleski? = Do you speak English? (the li-question comes on lesson 12)
Učim hrvatski. = I'm learning Croatian. Full row: učim, učiš, uči, učimo, učite, uče.

activities.title: Conjugation practice
activities.intro: Produce the right form for each person. The ending is everything: -m, -š, (none), -mo, -te, -e.

questions.prompt: ti + raditi = ti ___ (you work)
questions.explanation: ti takes -š: radiš. Don't forget the š.

questions.prompt: ja + učiti = ja ___ (I learn)
questions.explanation: ja takes -m: učim.

questions.prompt: oni + raditi = oni ___ (they work)
questions.explanation: -i verbs take -e in the they-form: rade. (-a verbs take -ju: gledaju.)

questions.prompt: mi + govoriti = mi ___ (we speak)
questions.explanation: mi takes -mo: govorimo.

questions.prompt: What does 'radite' mean?
questions.explanation: -te always marks vi: radite, govorite, učite. This is also the polite form for one person.

questions.prompt: 'Uči hrvatski.', who is learning?
questions.explanation: No ending after the stem (uči-) = third person singular. The bare form with no pronoun still tells you exactly who.

questions.prompt: Build the classic: 'I speak a little Croatian.'
questions.explanation: No ja needed, the -m says 'I'. malo = a little.

questions.prompt: Translate with a dropped pronoun: 'We work.' (one word)
questions.explanation: Radimo. one word is a complete, natural sentence. The -mo can only mean 'we'.

days.title: Core vocabulary: people & family
days.paretoFocus: Frequency-first vocabulary means every word is one you'll use; family is among the most-discussed topics.
drills: Learn: obitelj, majka, otac, brat, sestra, prijatelj…
Describe your family with imati (to have).
Drill today's words, then clear the Words tab.

activities.title: Family words
activities.intro: Family is one of the most-discussed topics in Croatia, you WILL be asked about yours. These are the core words; each is in its dictionary (nominative) form.

items.note: Feminine, despite ending in a consonant (like noć).
Informal: mama.
Informal: tata.
Colloquially often kćerka.
Plural is irregular: djeca (children).
Yes, the same word as 'woman', context decides. Moja žena = my wife.
Female friend: prijateljica.

activities.title: imati (to have), first look
activities.intro: To describe your family you need imati. It follows the -am pattern perfectly. One heads-up: after imati the noun is a direct object, so some endings shift (brat → brata, sestra → sestru). That pattern is lesson 11's lesson, for now, memorize the two phrases below as chunks.

items.note: -a verb, so 'they' takes -ju.

items.en: I have a brother and a sister.
items.note: Chunk to memorize: brat→brata, sestra→sestru because they're objects. You'll learn this pattern (the accusative) on lesson 11.
Ovo je... = This is... the easiest way to present people: Ovo je moj brat. Ovo je moja sestra.

activities.title: Family practice
activities.intro: Recognize the words, conjugate imati, and present your own family with Ovo je...

questions.prompt: What does 'sestra' mean?
questions.explanation: sestra = sister. brat = brother.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'brother'
questions.explanation: brat = brother.

questions.prompt: What does 'kći' mean?
questions.explanation: kći = daughter (colloquially kćerka). sin = son.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'mother'
questions.explanation: majka = mother; mama is the informal 'mum'.

questions.prompt: The plural of 'dijete' (child) is:
questions.explanation: Irregular: dijete → djeca. (djed = grandfather, a different word.)

questions.prompt: mi + imati = mi ___ (we have)
questions.explanation: imati follows the -am pattern: imam, imaš, ima, imamo, imate, imaju.

questions.prompt: Present her: 'This is my sister.'
questions.explanation: Ovo je + moja (my, feminine, matching sestra) + sestra.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'This is my brother.'
questions.explanation: moj (not moja) because brat is masculine. Possessives agree with the noun's gender: moj brat, moja sestra.

questions.prompt: Say the memorized chunk: 'I have a brother and a sister.'
questions.explanation: brat→brata and sestra→sestru because they are objects of imam, the accusative pattern you'll properly learn on lesson 11.

activities.title: Role-play: looking at a family photo
activities.intro: Sit down with your partner and a real family photo (yours or theirs). Act out the script, then keep going off-script: point and say Ovo je... for every person you can.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ovo je moja obitelj. Ovo je moja majka.
lines.en: This is my family. This is my mother.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: To je moj brat. Zove se Ivan.
lines.en: That's my brother. His name is Ivan.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: I ja imam brata. Zove se Tom.
lines.en: I have a brother too. His name is Tom.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Super! A ovo je moja baka.
lines.en: Great! And this is my grandmother.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ovo je moja sestra. Zove se Emma.
lines.en: This is my sister. Her name is Emma.

days.title: Nominative case (the subject)
days.objective: Understand the nominative as the dictionary/subject form.
days.paretoFocus: Nominative is the 'home base' of every noun, the form that DOES the action. Knowing what it is makes the other cases make sense.
drills: Identify the subject in 5 sentences.
Note nominative = the form you look up.
Make 5 'X is/does Y' sentences.

items: Point out the nominative in 3 sentences.

activities.title: The nominative: home base
activities.intro: Croatian nouns change their endings depending on their ROLE in a sentence, these forms are called cases. The nominative is case number one: the SUBJECT, the do-er, the form you look up in a dictionary. Every noun you've learned so far has been in the nominative. It answers the questions Tko? (who?) and Što? (what?).

items.en: the nominative, the dictionary form and the subject form
items.note: žena, grad, more as you learned them = nominative. It's the 'home base' every other case starts from.
Find the subject by asking: WHO does the action? The answer is in the nominative.
žena = subject = nominative.
Tko spava? dijete. Subject, nominative.
With biti, the subject stays nominative: grad.
The whole subject phrase (moj brat) is in the nominative.
Tko uči? Ana. Names are nouns too and take cases like any other.
Preview: žena = nominative (drinker), kavu = the OBJECT, kava changed to kavu. That object form is the accusative, tomorrow's lesson.
Because endings mark the roles, Croatian word order is flexible, žena is the drinker wherever it stands in the sentence.

activities.title: Find the subject
activities.intro: Ask Tko? or Što? and point at the nominative in each sentence.

questions.prompt: Which question words does the nominative answer?
questions.explanation: The nominative names the do-er: WHO or WHAT does the action.

questions.prompt: 'Žena pije kavu.', which word is the nominative subject?
questions.explanation: Tko pije? žena. kavu is the object (accusative, kava with a changed ending), pije is the verb.

questions.prompt: 'Moj brat radi.', who or what is the subject?
questions.explanation: Tko radi? (moj) brat. The noun brat is in the nominative; moj agrees with it.

questions.prompt: The nominative is also...
options: the form you look up in a dictionary
questions.answer: the form you look up in a dictionary
questions.explanation: Dictionary form = nominative singular. That's why it's home base: every other case is built from it.

questions.prompt: You see 'kavu' in a sentence. What is its dictionary (nominative) form?
questions.explanation: kavu is kava wearing its object ending. To find a word in the dictionary, restore the nominative: kava.

activities.title: Build 'X is/does Y' sentences
activities.intro: Now produce full sentences with the subject in the nominative, the pattern behind most of what you'll ever say.

questions.prompt: Build: 'The coffee is warm.'
questions.explanation: Subject (kava, nominative) + je + adjective. topla is feminine to match kava.

questions.prompt: Build: 'My brother works.'
questions.explanation: Subject phrase in the nominative (moj brat) + verb (radi).

questions.prompt: Translate: 'The child sleeps.'
questions.explanation: dijete (nominative, neuter) + spava (he/she/it form of spavati, to sleep).

questions.prompt: Word order twist: 'Kavu pije žena.', WHO is drinking?
questions.explanation: Endings beat word order: žena is nominative (the do-er) and kavu is the object, no matter which comes first. This flexibility is WHY cases exist.

questions.prompt: Translate: 'Ana is learning Croatian.'
questions.explanation: Ana = nominative subject; uči = the she-form of učiti; hrvatski = Croatian (the language).

days.title: Accusative case (the object)
days.objective: Use the accusative for the direct object.
days.paretoFocus: Nominative + accusative covers the vast majority of basic sentences ('I drink coffee'). This is the highest-leverage grammar in Croatian.
drills: Feminine -a → -u: kava → kavu.
Masculine inanimate stays the same.
Make 6 'I + verb + object' sentences.

activities.title: The accusative: nominative → object form
activities.intro: The accusative marks the direct object, the thing receiving the action ('I drink COFFEE'). The rules are tiny: feminine -a becomes -u, masculine inanimate and neuter nouns don't change at all, and masculine ANIMATE nouns (people, animals) add -a. Learn these real transformation pairs.

items.note: Feminine -a → -u: Pijem kavu. (I drink coffee.)
Pijem vodu. (I drink water.)
Čitam knjigu. (I am reading a book.)
Jedem ribu. (I am eating fish.)
Volim Hrvatsku. (I love Croatia.) Country names in -a follow the same rule.
Masculine INANIMATE stays the same: Vidim grad. (I see the city.)
Jedem kruh. (I am eating bread.) No change.
Pijem sok. (I drink juice.) No change.
Neuter (-o/-e) never changes: Pijem pivo.
Masculine ANIMATE adds -a: Vidim prijatelja. (I see a friend.)
Animate: Imam brata. (I have a brother.)
Animate + 'fleeting a': the a of pas drops → Imam psa. (I have a dog.)

activities.title: Accusative practice: I + verb + object
activities.intro: Turn nouns into their object form and build real 'I + verb + object' sentences.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I drink coffee'?
questions.explanation: kava is feminine, so as the direct object -a changes to -u: Pijem kavu.

questions.prompt: Complete with the accusative of 'knjiga': Čitam ___ .
questions.explanation: Feminine -a → -u: knjiga → knjigu. Čitam knjigu = I am reading a book.

questions.prompt: Which noun does NOT change in the accusative?
questions.explanation: grad is masculine inanimate, so the accusative looks exactly like the nominative: Vidim grad.

questions.prompt: Complete with the accusative of 'voda': Pijem ___ .
questions.explanation: voda → vodu (feminine -a → -u).

questions.prompt: Complete: Vidim ___ . ('I see my brother')
questions.explanation: brat is masculine ANIMATE, so the accusative adds -a: Vidim brata.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'The woman drinks coffee.'
questions.explanation: Žena (subject, nominative) pije kavu (object, accusative).

questions.prompt: Complete: Volim ___ . ('I love Croatia', Hrvatska)
questions.explanation: Hrvatska ends in -a, so the object form is Hrvatsku: Volim Hrvatsku.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I am eating a sandwich'?
questions.explanation: sendvič is masculine inanimate, it stays the same in the accusative.

questions.prompt: Complete: Imam ___ . ('I have a dog', pas)
questions.explanation: pas is animate (add -a) AND has a fleeting a that drops: pas → psa. Imam psa.

days.title: Negation & yes/no questions
days.objective: Make sentences negative and ask yes/no questions with 'li'.
days.paretoFocus: Negation (ne + verb) and the 'li' question double your range and let you start real exchanges.
drills: Negate 5 sentences with 'ne'.
Form 'Govoriš li engleski?'-style questions.
Turn three statements into questions using the 'verb + li' pattern.

activities.title: Saying no and asking yes/no questions
activities.intro: Negation is beautifully simple: put 'ne' directly before the verb. Only imati fuses into one word (nemam). For yes/no questions, put 'li' right after the verb: Govoriš li engleski? Master these patterns and your range doubles.

items.note: ne + verb, always directly before the verb.
Your most useful survival sentence.
ne + verb + accusative object.
Only imati fuses with ne: nemam, NEVER 'ne imam'.
biti also has fused negatives: nisam, nisi, nije… (lesson 6 review)
Yes/no question: verb + li + rest.
vi-form for strangers and groups.
Standard Croatian yes/no questions use 'verb + li' or 'je li'. The 'da li' form is Serbian and avoided in the Croatian standard.
Answering negatively doubles the ne: 'Ne,' (no) + 'ne govorim' (I don't speak).

activities.title: Negate it, question it
activities.intro: Practise 'ne' + verb, the fused 'nemam', and building li-questions.

questions.prompt: Make it negative: 'Govorim engleski.'
questions.explanation: ne stands directly before the verb, as a separate word.

questions.prompt: Say 'I don't understand.'
questions.explanation: ne + razumijem. The single most useful phrase for a learner.

questions.prompt: What is the negative of 'imam' (I have)?
questions.explanation: imati is the only verb that fuses with ne: nemam, nemaš, nema… 'ne imam' is wrong.

questions.prompt: Build the question: 'Do you speak English?'
questions.explanation: Yes/no question = verb first, then li, then the rest.

questions.prompt: Where does 'li' go in a yes/no question?
questions.explanation: li always comes immediately after the verb, never first, never last.

questions.prompt: Ask a friend 'Do you drink coffee?' (piti, informal)
questions.explanation: piješ (you drink) + li + kavu (accusative object).

questions.prompt: Which is the standard Croatian yes/no question?
questions.explanation: Standard Croatian forms yes/no questions with 'verb + li' (Govoriš li...?) or 'je li'. The 'da li' form is Serbian and is avoided in the Croatian standard.

questions.prompt: Answer 'Radiš li danas?' with NO:
questions.explanation: First 'Ne,' answers the question; then 'ne radim' negates the verb. Croatian happily doubles the ne.

days.title: Question words
days.objective: Use tko, što, gdje, kada, zašto, kako, koliko.
days.paretoFocus: Seven question words open every information-seeking sentence.
drills: Learn all 7 question words.
Ask: Gdje…? Kada…? Koliko…?
Build 7 questions, one per word.

items: Ask 'Where is the station?' (Gdje je kolodvor?)

activities.title: The seven question words (+ two bonus ones)
activities.intro: Seven little words open every information question in Croatian: tko and što ask about people and things, gdje and kada pin down place and time, zašto asks for reasons, kako for manner, and koliko for amount. Learn each one inside a real question you will actually ask.

items.note: Tko je to? Who is that?
Što radiš? What are you doing?
Gdje je kolodvor? Where is the station?
Kada radiš? When do you work? (also shortened: kad)
Zašto učiš hrvatski? Why are you learning Croatian?
Kako si? How are you?
Koliko košta? How much does it cost?
Bonus: Odakle si? Where are you from?

items.en: What is your name? (literally: how do you call yourself)
items.note: Croatian asks 'how', not 'what', for names.
Survival question number one when travelling.

activities.title: Ask the right question
activities.intro: Match each situation to the right question word and build full questions.

questions.prompt: Which word means 'where'?
questions.explanation: gdje = where. Gdje je kolodvor? Where is the station?

questions.prompt: Complete: ___ košta kava? (How much does the coffee cost?)
questions.explanation: koliko = how much/how many. Don't mix it up with kako (how).

questions.prompt: Complete: ___ se zoveš? (What's your name?)
questions.explanation: Croatian literally asks 'HOW do you call yourself': Kako se zoveš?

questions.prompt: Complete: ___ je to? To je Marko. (…is that? That's Marko.)
questions.explanation: The answer is a person, so the question word is tko (who). For things you would use što (what).

questions.prompt: Ask 'Where is the station?': ___ je kolodvor?
questions.explanation: gdje = where. Gdje je kolodvor?

questions.prompt: Which word means 'why'?
questions.explanation: zašto = why. Careful: zato = 'that's why / because of that', the answer, not the question!

questions.prompt: Build the question: 'How much does the beer cost?'
questions.explanation: Question word first: Koliko košta pivo?

questions.prompt: What does 'Što radiš?' mean?
questions.explanation: što = what, radiš = you do/are doing. A very common conversation opener.

days.title: A0 milestone check
days.objective: Prove you can read aloud, greet, introduce yourself and count.
days.paretoFocus: Milestone checks turn scattered knowledge into real capability and reveal exactly what to review.
drills: Take the A0 quiz in the app.
Read a short paragraph aloud cold.
Do a 3-minute self-introduction.

items: Complete the A0 Feynman: 'The Croatian alphabet'.

activities.title: Your A0 toolkit, everything in one place
activities.intro: Milestone day! These ten lines pack together everything from lessons 1-13: greetings, introducing yourself, biti, verbs, the accusative, negation and questions. If you can produce all ten from memory, you have reached A0.

items.note: Greeting + biti (lesson 2 + 6).

items.hr: Zovem se Alex. Drago mi je.
items.en: My name is Alex. Nice to meet you.
items.note: The polite full introduction (lesson 3).

items.hr: Odakle si? Ja sam iz Amerike.
items.en: Where are you from? I am from America.
items.note: 'iz + place' = from. Fixed phrase for now.
Present tense endings (lesson 8).
Politeness + negation (lesson 12).
One magic word when you miss something.

items.en: How much does it cost? Ten euros.
items.note: Question word + numbers (lessons 7 + 13).

items.hr: Pijem kavu, a on pije pivo.
items.en: I drink coffee, and he drinks beer.
items.note: Nominative subject + accusative object in action (lessons 10-11).
li-question (lesson 12).
Closing any interaction politely (lesson 2).

activities.title: A0 milestone quiz
activities.intro: A mixed check across everything so far: greetings, biti, genders, verbs, accusative, negation and questions. Aim for at least 8 out of 10.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'thank you'?
questions.explanation: hvala = thanks; molim = please / you're welcome.

questions.prompt: Complete: Ja ___ student. (I am a student.)
questions.explanation: Short form of biti: ja sam, ti si, on je…

questions.prompt: What gender is 'kava'?
questions.explanation: Nouns in -a are (almost always) feminine: kava, žena, škola.

questions.prompt: Conjugate: Mi ___ hrvatski. (govoriti, we speak)
questions.explanation: mi-form ends in -mo: govorimo.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'She is not here'?
questions.explanation: biti has fused negatives: nije = is not. 'ne je' does not exist.

questions.prompt: Complete with the object form: Pijem ___ . (kava)
questions.explanation: Accusative of kava is kavu, feminine -a → -u.

questions.prompt: Which number is 'petnaest'?
questions.explanation: pet = 5, petnaest = 15, pedeset = 50. The -naest numbers are the teens.

questions.prompt: Build the question: 'What is your name?'
questions.explanation: Kako se zoveš? literally 'How do you call yourself?'

questions.prompt: Ask 'Do you speak English?'
questions.explanation: Yes/no question: verb + li. (Without li it can work in speech, but the li-form is the one to master.)

questions.prompt: Say 'I don't understand.'
questions.explanation: ne + razumijem, negation goes directly before the verb.

activities.title: Milestone conversation: meeting Ivana
activities.intro: Your first full conversation in Croatian, a real exchange using only what you've learned in the first two weeks. Read your lines ('Vi') aloud; better yet, cover the English and check yourself.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Ja sam Ivana. Kako se zovete?
lines.en: Good day! I am Ivana. What is your name? (formal)

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dobar dan! Zovem se Alex. Drago mi je.
lines.en: Good day! My name is Alex. Nice to meet you.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Nice to meet you. Where are you from?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ja sam iz Amerike. Sada živim u Zagrebu.
lines.en: I am from America. Now I live in Zagreb.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Govorim malo hrvatski. Učim svaki dan.
lines.en: I speak a little Croatian. I study every day.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Da, volim kavu. Pijem kavu svaki dan.
lines.en: Yes, I love coffee. I drink coffee every day.

days.title: Ovo, to i ono: pokazivanje
days.objective: Point at things near and far and ask what they are using ovo, to and ono.
days.paretoFocus: Pointing words let you shop, ask and identify anything before you know its name, so they multiply every noun you learn.
drills: Point at five objects around you and say Ovo je... for each one.
Ask Što je ovo? about something close and Što je ono? about something far away.
Ask one yes/no question with Je li to...? about an object in the room.

items: Say 'This is my book' and 'That over there is our house'.
Ask 'Is that your bag?' as a yes/no question.

activities.title: Ovo, to, ono
activities.intro: Croatian has three pointing words for three distances: ovo for something near me, to for something near you, and ono for something far from both of us. With Što je...? you can ask what anything is.

items.note: ovo = this, near me
to = that, near you

items.en: That over there is our house.
items.note: ono = that, far from both of us
yes/no question: Je li...?

activities.title: Pokaži i reci
activities.intro: Choose the right pointing word for each distance and build simple questions about objects.

questions.prompt: You are pointing at a building far away from both of you. Complete: ___ je hotel.
questions.explanation: ono points at something far from both speakers; ovo is only for things near you, the speaker.

questions.prompt: Ask what the object in your hand is: ___ je ovo?
questions.explanation: Što je ovo? is the basic question for identifying things. Što means what.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct yes/no question for 'Is this a museum?'
questions.explanation: Standard Croatian yes/no questions use Je li...? The second option opens with a question phrase that is not standard Croatian, and it is also missing the verb.

questions.prompt: Your friend is holding a jacket. Complete: ___ je tvoja jakna. (that, near the listener)
questions.explanation: For something near the person you are speaking with, Croatian uses to.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'This is my house.'
questions.explanation: Ovo starts the sentence, then the verb je, then moja kuća with the possessive before the noun.

activities.title: U novom stanu
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are showing a friend around your new apartment and pointing at things with ovo, to and ono.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A ono? Je li ono tvoja soba?
lines.en: And that over there? Is that your room?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes, that over there is my room.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Hvala! To je poklon od bake.
lines.en: Thank you! It is a present from my grandma.

days.title: Veliki brojevi: sto, tisuća, milijun
days.objective: Understand and say prices and other amounts above one hundred with sto, tisuća and milijun.
days.paretoFocus: Rents, salaries and prices all live above one hundred; without these three words you cannot handle money in Croatia.
drills: Count aloud by hundreds: sto, dvjesto, tristo... up to devetsto.
Say three realistic prices in euros, for a coffee, a phone and a holiday.
Say how many people live in a big city, using milijun.

items: Say the Croatian words for one hundred, one thousand and one million.

activities.title: Sto, tisuća, milijun
activities.intro: Croatian builds hundreds by stacking sto: dvjesto, tristo, četiristo. A thousand is tisuća and a million is milijun; after a verb like košta you will hear the form tisuću.

items.note: Sto eura. A hundred euros.

items.en: two hundred, three hundred, five hundred
items.note: hundreds are written as one word: četiristo, šeststo, sedamsto...
To košta tisuću eura. It costs a thousand euros.
milijun ljudi = a million people
Answer: Košta sto pedeset eura.

activities.title: Koliko košta?
activities.intro: Practise the words for hundreds, thousands and millions in real prices.

questions.prompt: Choose the standard Croatian word for 'a thousand'.
questions.explanation: The standard Croatian word is tisuća; the second option is not a standard Croatian word at all. tisuću and tisuće are real forms of tisuća, but not its basic form.

questions.prompt: Write the number 100 as a Croatian word.
questions.explanation: 100 = sto. It combines into dvjesto, tristo and so on.

questions.prompt: Complete: U Zagrebu živi gotovo ___ ljudi. (a million)
questions.explanation: Standard Croatian is milijun, with j; the spelling without it is not standard Croatian. milijuna would follow a number like dva.

questions.prompt: Complete the price of the trip: Putovanje košta dvije ___ eura. (thousand)
questions.explanation: After dvije, the noun tisuća takes the form tisuće: dvije tisuće eura.

questions.prompt: Build the Croatian for: 'It costs a hundred and fifty euros.'
questions.explanation: To košta + the number: sto pedeset, then eura for the currency.

activities.title: U trgovini tehnike
activities.intro: Play 'Me'. You are asking about prices in an electronics shop, using numbers above one hundred.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.en: Good afternoon! Do you need help?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: Yes. How much does this mobile phone cost?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.en: And how much does that computer over there cost?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: To je puno novca. Hvala lijepa!
lines.en: That is a lot of money. Thank you very much!
